verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: three homilies concerning the power of demons.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: introduction by rev. w. r. w. stephens.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: The three following Homilies are closely connected in subject, and the opening sentence of the third clearly proves that it was delivered two days after the second; but it is impossible to say whether that which is placed first was really delivered before the other two.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: It must however have been spoken at Antioch, since Chrysostom refers at the beginning of it to his sermons “on the obscurity of prophecies” in which passages occur which clearly imply that he was not then a Bishop.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: The second of the three homilies here translated was delivered in the presence of a Bishop, as is clearly indicated by the commencement, and as the third was as already mentioned delivered two days after the second we may safely affirm that they were all spoken at Antioch when Chrysostom was a presbyter there under the Episcopate of Flavian.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: They deal with errors against which Chrysostom throughout his life most strenuously contended.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: In an age of great depravity there seem to have been many who tried to excuse the weak resistance which they made to evil, both in themselves, and in others, by maintaining that the world was abandoned to the dominion of devils, or to the irresistible course of fate.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: To counteract the disastrous effects of such philosophy, which surrendered man to the current of his passions, it was necessary to insist very boldly and resolutely on the essential freedom of the will, on moral responsibility, and the duty of vigorous exertion in resisting temptation.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: And Chrysostom did this to an extent which some thought carried him perilously near the errors of the Pelagian heresy.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: No one however has described in more forcible language the powerful hold of sin upon human nature, and the insufficiency of man to shake it off without the assistance of divine grace.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: What he does most earnestly combat, both in the following homilies and very many others, is the doctrine that evil was an original integral part of our nature: he maintains that it is not a substantial inherent force (δύναμις ἐνυπόστατος).

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: If evil was a part of our nature in this sense it would be no more reprehensible than natural appetites and affections.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: We do not try to alter that which is by nature (φύσει): sin therefore is not by nature, because by means of education, laws, and punishments we do seek to alter that.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: Sin comes through defect in the moral purpose (προαίρεσις).

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: Our first parents fell through indolence of moral purpose (ῥ‹θυμία) and this is the principal cause of sin now.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: They marked out a path which has been trodden ever since: the force of will has been weakened in all their posterity: so that though evil is not an inherent part of man’s nature yet he is readily inclined to it (ὀξυρῥεπὴς πρὸς κακι€ν); and this tendency must be perpetually counteracted by vigorous exertion, and a bracing up of the moral purpose, with the aid of divine grace.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: Profoundly convinced therefore on the one hand of a strong and universal tendency to sin, but on the other of an essential freedom of the will, Chrysostom sounds alternately the note of warning and encouragement,—warning against that weakness, indolence, languor of moral purpose which occasions a fall,—encouragement to use to the full all the powers with which man is gifted, in reliance on God’s forbearance and love, and on His willingness to help those who do not despair of themselves.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: Despair is the devil’s most potent instrument for effecting the ruin of man; for it is that which prevents him from rising again after he has fallen.

verses.book: Three Homilies Concerning the Power of Demons.
verses.text: St. Paul repented, and, not despairing, became equal to angels: Judas repenting, but despairing, rushed into perdition.
